How to get a refund: 1) SCREENSHOT immediately — listed price, cart, final receipt 2) Email the vendor: 'Your advertised rate was $X. My mandatory total was $Y. cont_clean: A major misconception is that the at-fault party's insurance company will pay your medical bills as they roll in. Unfortunately, that's not how it works. They only pay out in one lump sum after a final settlement is reached. So, how do you cover your treatments in the meantime? Here are the 3 most common ways: Your Health Insurance: You can use your own health insurance (private, Medicare, or Medicaid) to cover upfront costs. Keep in mind, they will likely seek reimbursement (subrogation) from your final settlement later. Medical Payments Coverage (MedPay): If you have MedPay on your own auto insurance policy, it can be used immediately to pay for medical bills up to your policy limit, regardless of who caused the accident. Medical Liens: Many medical providers understand the legal process and will agree to treat you on a "lien." This means they provide care now and agree to wait for payment out of your final case settlement. That distinction matters. Survivors and creators deserve to have control over their own images, privacy, and safety. The law also places responsibility on covered platforms to have clear processes in place when reports are made. Platforms that allow user uploaded content should take this seriously, review their responsibilities, and ensure they are prepared to respond quickly and appropriately. For anyone whose intimate image or video has been shared without consent, this law creates a federal process that may make removal faster and more accessible. As platforms begin implementing these requirements, it can be helpful to document reports, save confirmation emails or screenshots, and keep records of where content appeared. cont_clean: The FDA has approved a new over-the-counter naloxone nasal spray for opioid overdose. On 2 July 2026, the FDA cleared Rextovy, a 4 mg naloxone hydrochloride nasal spray. It can now be bought without a prescription. Same active ingredient as the naloxone sprays already on shelves. Same dose. No new clinical benefit. That is the point. Regulators usually approve one product for a use, and the market takes over from there. Naloxone is different. Every new approval means one more company competing for the same shelf. More competition brings prices down. Lower prices put the spray in more hands. In an overdose, minutes decide whether someone lives, so access matters more than novelty. The FDA said as much in its announcement. More formulations mean wider availability, more competition, and lower cost. For pharma teams, there is a lesson here. Life-saving generics are being treated as public health tools, not just commercial products. The FDA is using approvals to shape the market, not only to police it. Companies working on other over-the-counter transitions, such as diagnostic tests, glucose monitors, and mental health apps, should pay attention. This is a new model, and it may not stop with naloxone. The story is not that the product exists. The story is that the FDA wants many like it to exist. cont_clean: 🚨 PFAS in Breast Milk: A Silent Warning We Can't Ignore Breast milk is nature's first source of nutrition, designed to give babies the healthiest possible start. Yet research has shown that PFAS ("forever chemicals") have been detected in breast milk around the world, reflecting how widespread these persistent chemicals have become. PFAS accumulate in the human body over time and can be transferred from mother to child during breastfeeding. Scientists are studying how early-life exposure may affect a baby's developing immune system, growth, and long-term health. While the evidence continues to evolve, reducing PFAS exposure has become an important public health goal. This is not a reason to avoid breastfeeding. Health authorities continue to recommend breastfeeding because its well-established benefits far outweigh the known risks. Instead, the findings highlight a much bigger issue: PFAS pollution is so pervasive that it has reached one of the most fundamental sources of infant nutrition. The real solution isn't for mothers to change, it is for governments, industries, and communities to reduce PFAS contamination at its source, protect drinking water, and phase out unnecessary uses of these "forever chemicals." Every child deserves a healthier start to life. cont_clean: President Donald Trump signed an executive order May 12 that he said would lower prescription drug costs — an issue the U.S. bishops have flagged in recent years — but the exact potential impact of the order was the subject of debate. Trump signed an executive order to implement what is known as the "most favored nation" policy, which he said would tie the prices of some drugs in the U.S. to lower prices abroad, such as in Europe. "Basically, what we're doing is equalizing," Trump said at an event in the Roosevelt Room of the White House. "We are going to pay the lowest price there is in the world. We will get whoever is paying the lowest price, that's the price that we're going to get." The order sets a 30-day deadline for drug companies to voluntarily lower their prices, asking that the U.S. pay the same amount that other wealthy countries pay for similar medications, and offers the White House's support for these pharmaceutical companies to seek higher prices abroad. Officials said they would pursue other regulatory options if the companies did not comply. Stephen J. Ubl, PhRMA's chief executive, argued in a statement, "To lower costs for Americans, we need to address the real reasons U.S. prices are higher: foreign countries not paying their fair share and middlemen driving up prices for U.S. patients." "The Administration is right to use trade negotiations to force foreign governments to pay their fair share for medicines. U.S. patients should not foot the bill for global innovation," Ubl said. Americans pay more for insulin than patients in any other developed country. A 2021 RAND Corporation study found that U.S. insulin prices were nearly ten times higher than in 32 other high-income nations. The three dominant insulin manufacturers — Eli Lilly, Novo Nordisk, and Sanofi — control approximately 90% of the global insulin market. For decades, those companies raised insulin list prices in coordinated, near-simultaneous increments — a practice that drew FTC scrutiny. The Inflation Reduction Act of 2022 capped insulin costs at $35 per month for Medicare beneficiaries. It did not cap prices for patients with private insurance or no insurance. All three manufacturers later voluntarily capped their insulin prices at $35 per month broadly — under significant congressional and public pressure. Before those caps, Americans were rationing insulin. A documented and studied phenomenon: patients skipping doses, splitting pens, crossing into Canada with coolers to buy a month's supply. cont_clean: It's Day 32 / 90 of the #privacy101challenge. Is it necessary for schools to collect kids' biometrics? As schools are using technology to improve the quality of education, there are certain privacy concerns that come with it. Biometrics which include fingerprints, facial recognition and iris scans are being used in schools for taking attendance, lunch payments amongst other things. There are good reasons for using biometrics, one of which is unlike a student ID card, biometrics cannot be used by someone else. So no false attendance or taking someone else's lunch. However, weighing the pros and the cons, the cons far outweigh the pros. Before relying on using biometrics, it's important for technical measures to be put in place to prevent any data breach but schools lack this high level of security. Biometrics are the permanent identity of a person. They can never be changed. The Town of Groton has retained Daigle Law Group to investigate an internal affairs complaint filed by First Amendment auditor SeanPaul Reyes following his July 2025 arrest outside the home of retired Connecticut State Police Sgt. Bryan Fahey. The incident drew national attention after video appeared to show Fahey, while armed, spitting in Reyes's face during the confrontation. Reyes originally faced multiple criminal charges. Most have since been dismissed, leaving a single charge of third-degree criminal trespass pending. Fahey was later charged with disorderly conduct. Both cases remain before the court. Now comes the internal investigation. An outside law firm can absolutely provide an objective review. It can also become another layer between government officials and public accountability. That's why the public shouldn't simply assume "outside counsel" means independence. Who determines the scope of the investigation? Who decides what evidence investigators receive? Can investigators interview anyone they believe is relevant, or only those the town identifies? Does Groton Police get an opportunity to dispute or edit factual findings before the report is released? Will taxpayers receive the full report, or only a heavily redacted version? Those answers matter far more than the firm's name. If taxpayers are paying for this investigation, they deserve complete transparency, including the engagement letter, investigative scope, billing records, body camera footage, dispatch audio, evidence preservation instructions, internal communications, and the final report. Accountability isn't created by outsourcing an investigation. It's created by allowing the evidence-not the agency being investigated-to determine the outcome. #GrotonCT #PoliceAccountability #FirstAmendment #PublicRecords #CourtWatch

cont_clean: The Monopoly Was Legal. Congress Made It That Way. In most American communities, you have one cable company. Not because the market chose it. Because local governments granted exclusive franchise agreements. Cable companies negotiated franchise agreements with cities and counties that gave them exclusive rights to operate in a territory. In exchange, they agreed to build out infrastructure and pay franchise fees. The result: geographic monopolies with no competitive pressure on price or service quality. The FCC attempted to encourage competition through open-access requirements. Cable companies challenged and largely defeated those requirements in court. In 2024, the FCC attempted to restore its authority over broadband providers under Title II of the Communications Act. The rule was immediately challenged. A federal court struck it down later that year — limiting the FCC's ability to enforce net neutrality or impose service quality requirements. In the markets where a single cable provider operates, prices for broadband service in the United States are among the highest in the developed world. The infrastructure was often built with public rights-of-way. The monopoly that operates on it was granted by the government. The rates are set by the company. Follow Off Limits America. cont_clean: Free school meals have existed in some form in the UK for over a century, but eligibility has always been restricted to the most financially stretched households. Under the previous system, children in Universal Credit households only qualified if their household take home pay after tax and National Insurance was £7,400 a year or less. That threshold was widely criticised as too low, catching only the very poorest families while leaving out large numbers of households who were still genuinely struggling. From September 2026, that threshold is gone entirely for Universal Credit households. Every child whose family receives Universal Credit will now qualify for free school meals, regardless of how much the household earns on top of their benefit entitlement. The change was announced as part of the 2025 Budget and is expected to extend free school meals to around 700,000 additional children who were previously excluded purely because their household income sat above the £7,400 line. For families affected, the saving is real and immediate. Free school meals are worth around £500 per child per year based on current meal costs. For a family with two children both previously ineligible, that's up to £1,000 a year back in the household budget. The policy sits within a broader set of measures the government has described as part of its mission to reduce child poverty, alongside the scrapping of the two child benefit cap from April 2026 and the above inflation rise in Universal Credit standard allowances. Critics of the extension have questioned the cost at a time when the public finances are under significant pressure, and whether extending eligibility to working families on Universal Credit, some of whom earn significant incomes on top of their benefit entitlement, represents the most targeted use of the available funding. Supporters point out that food poverty and hunger in schools has been a documented problem affecting children's ability to learn and concentrate, and that the previous £7,400 threshold created an arbitrary cliff edge #UKPolitics #FreeSchoolMeals #ChildPoverty #PersonalFinance #KnowTheRules

cont_clean: Burnout doesn't just take your energy. That's usually where it starts—but it's rarely where it ends. First, you notice you're tired more often. Then concentrating becomes harder. Tasks that used to take minutes take twice as long. The things you once enjoyed feel like obligations. You start canceling plans, withdrawing from people you care about, and telling yourself you'll reconnect when things calm down. Eventually, the most valuable thing begins to disappear: your time. Not just the hours spent working, but the moments that make life feel meaningful—the dinner you weren't fully present for, the hobby you stopped making time for, the conversations you were too exhausted to have, the memories that never got created because work consumed the space they needed. That's why burnout is about more than productivity. Productivity can recover. Lost energy can return.
